1.lecture:
§1 · Phases, components, and equilibrium

A phase is a region of uniform structure and composition — solid, liquid, or a distinct crystal type; a component is a pure substance making up the alloy. A phase diagram maps, for an alloy of two components, which phases are present and in what amounts at each temperature and overall composition, assuming equilibrium. It is the metallurgist's master chart: from it you read melting behaviour, what forms on cooling, and how heat treatment will change the microstructure. The regions are bounded by the liquidus (above which all is liquid) and the solidus (below which all is solid); between them, solid and liquid coexist.

§2 · The tie line and the lever rule

Inside a two-phase region, a point on the diagram tells you three things through a horizontal tie line drawn at that temperature. Its ends, where it meets the phase boundaries, give the compositions of the two phases — the liquid \( C_L \) and the solid \( C_\alpha \). The lever rule then gives the amounts: the fraction of each phase is the length of the opposite lever arm over the whole tie line, \( W_L=\dfrac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L} \) and \( W_\alpha=\dfrac{C_0-C_L}{C_\alpha-C_L} \). For an overall composition \( C_0=40 \)% between \( C_L=30 \)% and \( C_\alpha=50 \)%: \( W_L=\dfrac{50-40}{50-30}=0.5 \), so the alloy is half liquid and half solid by mass. The nearer \( C_0 \) sits to one boundary, the more of that phase there is.

Key tool — the lever rule

\[ W_L=\frac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L},\qquad W_\alpha=\frac{C_0-C_L}{C_\alpha-C_L}\quad(W_L+W_\alpha=1). \]

§3 · Binary systems: isomorphous and eutectic

Two patterns cover most binary alloys. An isomorphous system (like copper–nickel) has complete solid solubility — one solid phase across all compositions — and a simple lens-shaped two-phase region between liquidus and solidus. A eutectic system (like lead–tin, or the iron–carbon system of the next lesson) has limited solubility and a special eutectic point: the lowest-melting composition, where liquid transforms directly into two solids at a single temperature, \( L\to\alpha+\beta \). The eutectic reaction gives the fine two-phase microstructures that control the strength of solders, cast alloys, and steels — which is why the eutectic and eutectoid points are the landmarks a metallurgist memorises.

§4 · The phase rule and the method

How many variables you may change freely is fixed by the Gibbs phase rule, \( F=C-P+2 \), or at constant pressure \( F=C-P+1 \). For a binary alloy (\(C=2\)) in a single-phase region (\(P=1\)), \( F=2 \): temperature and composition both vary freely. In a two-phase region (\(P=2\)), \( F=1 \): fix the temperature and both phase compositions are pinned by the tie-line ends. The working method for reading any phase diagram: (1) locate the point by temperature and overall composition; (2) identify the phase(s) present; (3) if two phases, draw the tie line for their compositions and apply the lever rule for their amounts. This single procedure unlocks every alloy chart in the course.

quiz.q:
Lever rule

Problem. At a temperature where the liquid is \(C_L=30\)% B and the solid is \(C_\alpha=50\)% B, an alloy of overall composition \(C_0=40\)% B sits in the two-phase field. Find the fraction of each phase.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( W_L=\dfrac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L}=\dfrac{50-40}{50-30}=\dfrac{10}{20}=0.5 \) and \( W_\alpha=\dfrac{C_0-C_L}{C_\alpha-C_L}=\dfrac{10}{20}=0.5 \). The alloy is 50% liquid, 50% solid by mass.

quiz.q:
Off-centre composition

Problem. For the same tie line (\(C_L=30\), \(C_\alpha=50\)) but an overall composition \(C_0=45\)% B, find the phase fractions.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( W_L=\dfrac{50-45}{50-30}=\dfrac{5}{20}=0.25 \), so \( W_\alpha=0.75 \). Because \(C_0\) is closer to the solid boundary, the alloy is now three-quarters solid.

quiz.q:
Degrees of freedom

Problem. Using the Gibbs phase rule at constant pressure, find the degrees of freedom for a binary alloy in (a) a single-phase field and (b) a two-phase field.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( F=C-P+1 \) with \(C=2\). (a) One phase: \( F=2-1+1=2 \) — temperature and composition both free. (b) Two phases: \( F=2-2+1=1 \) — fixing temperature pins both phase compositions via the tie line.

quiz.q: The lever-rule fraction of liquid is:
quiz.solution: Fraction of a phase = opposite lever arm ÷ whole tie line: \( W_L=\frac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L} \).

quiz.q: A tie line is used to find:
choices: the compositions of the two phases
quiz.solution: Its ends on the phase boundaries give the two phase compositions; the lever rule then gives amounts.

quiz.q: A eutectic reaction is:
quiz.solution: Liquid transforms directly into two solids at the eutectic temperature — the lowest-melting point.

quiz.q: For \(C_0=40\) between \(C_L=30\) and \(C_\alpha=50\), the alloy is:
The Gibbs phase rule at constant pressure is:
quiz.solution: At fixed pressure the general \( F=C-P+2 \) loses one variable, giving \( F=C-P+1 \).

quiz.q: An isomorphous system has:
quiz.solution: Like Cu–Ni, it forms a single solid solution at all compositions — a simple lens diagram.

quiz.q: In a single-phase region of the diagram, a phase's composition is:
quiz.solution: With only one phase present, that phase is the whole alloy, so its composition equals \(C_0\).

1.kuwait:
Phase diagrams are the daily reference of a foundry or steel-mill metallurgist. The iron–carbon diagram of the next lesson tells the melt shop what will solidify from a given carbon level and what heat treatment can later achieve; the lever rule quantifies how much of each phase — and therefore how hard or ductile the product will be. Reading a casting's composition onto the diagram predicts its structure before a sample is ever cut.

The same tool guides solder and brazing choices in fabrication and electronics work. The eutectic composition melts at the lowest, sharpest temperature — ideal for a clean joint that freezes quickly — while off-eutectic alloys freeze over a range, useful for wiping and filling. Selecting a filler is choosing a point on a phase diagram, trading melting behaviour against strength exactly as the lever rule and eutectic landmarks describe.

2.lecture:
§1 · The phases of steel

Steel is iron with up to about \(2\)% carbon, and its behaviour is the story of a few phases. Ferrite (\( \alpha \), body-centred cubic) is soft, ductile, and dissolves almost no carbon (\(0.022\)% maximum). Austenite (\( \gamma \), face-centred cubic) is the high-temperature phase and dissolves far more carbon (up to \(2.14\)%). Cementite (Fe\(_3\)C) is an iron carbide — hard and brittle, \(6.7\)% carbon. The workhorse microstructure is pearlite, a fine lamellar mixture of ferrite and cementite that forms when austenite is cooled slowly. Which phases you get, and in what proportions, is read directly from the iron–carbon diagram.

§2 · The eutectoid reaction

The landmark of the steel diagram is the eutectoid point at \(0.76\)% carbon and \(727^\circ\)C. There, on cooling, solid austenite transforms into two solids at once — \( \gamma\to\alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \) — producing pearlite. A steel of exactly \(0.76\)% carbon (eutectoid steel) becomes fully pearlitic. A hypoeutectoid steel (less carbon) first forms soft proeutectoid ferrite above \(727^\circ\)C, then the remaining austenite turns to pearlite; a hypereutectoid steel (more carbon) first forms cementite. The eutectoid reaction is the hinge on which all plain-carbon steel microstructures turn.

§3 · Reading amounts with the lever rule

The lever rule of Lesson 1 quantifies the microstructure. For a \(0.40\)% carbon (hypoeutectoid) steel just below \(727^\circ\)C, using \(C_\alpha=0.022\)% and the eutectoid \(0.76\)%: the proeutectoid ferrite fraction is \( \dfrac{0.76-0.40}{0.76-0.022}\approx0.49 \) and the pearlite fraction \( \dfrac{0.40-0.022}{0.76-0.022}\approx0.51 \). So a medium-carbon steel is roughly half soft ferrite and half pearlite — a balance of ductility and strength. More carbon means more pearlite (and eventually cementite), which is why hardness and strength climb, and ductility falls, with carbon content.

Key landmark — the eutectoid

\[ \gamma\ (0.76\%\ \text{C})\xrightarrow{727^\circ\text{C}}\ \alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C}\ (\text{pearlite}). \]

§4 · Heat treatment: beyond equilibrium

The diagram assumes slow, equilibrium cooling — but the power of steel comes from breaking that assumption. Cool austenite fast (quench) and carbon has no time to diffuse out; the result is martensite, a hard, brittle, distorted structure that gives steel its strength. Because martensite is too brittle to use as-quenched, it is tempered — reheated moderately — to trade some hardness for toughness. How readily a steel forms martensite at depth is its hardenability, improved by alloying (next lessons). The tools for predicting non-equilibrium structures are the TTT and CCT diagrams, which plot transformation against time; the working method is to pick a composition for the carbon it provides, then a cooling path (anneal, normalize, quench-and-temper) for the structure and properties you need.

Microstructure by the lever rule

Problem. For a \(0.40\)% carbon steel cooled slowly to just below \(727^\circ\)C, estimate the fractions of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite (use \(C_\alpha=0.022\)%, eutectoid \(0.76\)%).
quiz.solution: Solution. Proeutectoid ferrite \( =\dfrac{0.76-0.40}{0.76-0.022}=\dfrac{0.36}{0.738}\approx0.49 \); pearlite \( =\dfrac{0.40-0.022}{0.738}\approx0.51 \). About half ferrite, half pearlite.

quiz.q:
Steel designation

Problem. What carbon content does AISI 1040 steel contain, and what structure would slow cooling give it?
quiz.solution: Solution. In the AISI 10xx plain-carbon series the last two digits are the carbon in hundredths of a percent, so 1040 is \(0.40\)% C — a hypoeutectoid steel. Slow cooling gives a mix of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite (about 49%/51%, as above).

quiz.q:
Eutectoid steel

Problem. What is special about a \(0.76\)% carbon steel, and what does it become on slow cooling?
quiz.solution: Solution. It is the eutectoid composition. On cooling through \(727^\circ\)C its austenite transforms entirely by \( \gamma\to\alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \), so it becomes \(100\)% pearlite — no proeutectoid ferrite or cementite.

quiz.solution: The eutectoid point is at \(0.76\)% carbon, \(727^\circ\)C.

quiz.q: Austenite (γ) is:
quiz.solution: Austenite is face-centred cubic and dissolves far more carbon than ferrite.

quiz.q: Cementite (Fe₃C) is:
quiz.solution: Cementite is a hard, brittle iron carbide (6.7% C).

quiz.q: Pearlite is:
quiz.solution: Pearlite is the lamellar ferrite + cementite structure from the eutectoid reaction.

quiz.q: Quenching austenite rapidly produces:
quiz.solution: Fast cooling traps carbon and forms hard, brittle martensite.

quiz.q: Tempering a quenched steel:
quiz.solution: Reheating martensite moderately relieves brittleness — some hardness is lost, toughness is gained.

quiz.q: Increasing carbon content in plain-carbon steel generally:
choices: raises strength and hardness, lowers ductility
quiz.solution: More carbon means more pearlite/cementite — harder and stronger but less ductile.

quiz.q: A \(0.40\)% carbon steel slow-cooled below \(727^\circ\)C is about:
quiz.solution: Lever rule gives ~49% proeutectoid ferrite and ~51% pearlite.

2.kuwait:
The iron–carbon diagram and its heat treatments are the core knowledge of a steel mill and any heat-treat shop. Choosing a carbon level sets the achievable hardness; choosing a cooling path — full anneal for softness and machinability, normalize for a uniform structure, quench-and-temper for strength with toughness — sets the final properties. A rebar, a gear, and a spring may start from similar steel and diverge entirely through heat treatment, all predicted by this one diagram.

Getting the treatment wrong is a real failure mode maintenance sees. An over-quenched part left untempered is glass-hard and cracks in service; an under-hardened shaft wears and fatigues early. Reading a failed component's microstructure back against the iron–carbon diagram — was it pearlitic, martensitic, over-tempered? — is standard failure analysis, telling the investigator whether the metal or the process was at fault.

3.lecture:
§1 · Softening and homogenizing: anneal, normalize, stress relieve, spheroidize

Four heat treatments share one purpose — leave the steel softer, more uniform, or less stressed than it arrived — and differ only in how far they go. A full anneal austenitizes the steel, then cools it slowly in the furnace itself. The long time spent transforming near 727°C lets carbon diffuse far, so the pearlite that forms is coarse and widely spaced — the softest, most ductile condition the steel can reach, at the cost of hours of furnace time. A normalize austenitizes the same way but cools in still air — faster, cheaper, and stopping the transformation before carbon has time to spread as far, giving fine pearlite: moderately stronger and harder than annealed, and — because austenitizing resets the grain size — more uniform than as-cast or as-forged stock.

§2 · Reading the diagram — cooling rate sets lamellar spacing

The working method is the same tool from Lessons 1–2, run twice. Both curves leave the austenitizing temperature together and cross the 727°C eutectoid line — the furnace path far later than the air path. Time spent in the transformation range controls how far carbon can diffuse before the pearlite locks in: slow crossing → carbon travels far → thick, widely-spaced lamellae → soft. Fast crossing → carbon travels little → thin, closely-spaced lamellae → hard. This is the same principle as grain-size strengthening from Materials I: finer internal spacing resists dislocation motion more, so hardness rises as the microstructure gets finer — whether that spacing is grain size or lamellar spacing.

Stress relief and spheroidize stay below 727°C entirely, so neither re-forms austenite. Stress relief (roughly 600–650°C, held then air- or furnace-cooled) exists only to let locked-in residual stress from welding, machining, or cold forming relax — it changes no phase, only removes distortion risk. Spheroidize holds a high-carbon steel just under 727°C long enough that the cementite lamellae ball up into spheroids dispersed in ferrite — the softest, most formable condition a high-carbon steel reaches, used before heavy cold work such as cold heading or wire drawing.

§3 · Case hardening: a hard shell, a tough core

A gear tooth or a cam lobe needs a wear-resistant surface and a fracture-resistant core at once — through-hardening the whole part would win the surface and lose the core to brittleness. Three processes solve this by hardening only a shell. Carburizing diffuses carbon into a low-carbon steel's surface at high temperature (roughly 900–950°C) in a carbon-rich atmosphere; the enriched surface then hardens fully on quenching while the low-carbon core stays tough. Because it is diffusion, case depth follows the same square-root-of-time law as any diffusion process: \( x\approx k\sqrt{t} \) — doubling the case depth takes four times the time, not twice.

Nitriding diffuses nitrogen into the surface at a much lower temperature (roughly 500–550°C), forming hard nitride compounds directly, with no quench afterward — the part was already finish-machined, so avoiding a quench avoids the distortion a quench would cause. Induction hardening diffuses nothing at all: a coil heats only a thin surface layer of an already carbon-adequate steel above the transformation temperature in seconds, and that layer alone is quenched — fast, atmosphere-free, well suited to high-volume shafts and gears.

§4 · Choosing a treatment — the working method

Given a required property set, the sequence runs: (1) normalize or anneal the as-received bar stock so it machines cleanly and predictably; (2) machine to near-final shape; (3) case-harden the specific wear surfaces — carburizing where a deep case and a soft core both matter, induction hardening where speed and a defined shallow zone matter, nitriding where the part must not distort — leaving stock on the hardened surfaces for finishing, since hardening shifts dimensions slightly; (4) finish-grind the hardened surfaces to final size. A drawing callout that reads "case-harden journals, 0.8–1.0 mm effective case depth" is asking for exactly this sequence, in this order.

Key tool — diffusion case depth

\[ x \approx k\sqrt{t}\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad \frac{t_2}{t_1}=\left(\frac{x_2}{x_1}\right)^2. \]

quiz.q:
Normalized 1060 steel — lever rule reused

Problem. A 1060 steel (\(0.60\)% C) is normalized. Is it hypoeutectoid or hypereutectoid, and what fractions of ferrite and pearlite does it contain just below 727°C? (Use \(C_\alpha=0.022\)%, eutectoid \(0.76\)%.)
quiz.solution: Solution. \(0.60 < 0.76\), so it is hypoeutectoid. \( W_\alpha=\dfrac{0.76-0.60}{0.76-0.022}=\dfrac{0.16}{0.738}\approx0.217 \); \( W_{\text{pearlite}}=\dfrac{0.60-0.022}{0.738}\approx0.783 \). About 22% proeutectoid ferrite, 78% pearlite — and because it was normalized (air-cooled), that pearlite is fine, not coarse.

quiz.q:
Carburizing time for a deeper case

Problem. A carburizing cycle of 4 hours produces a 0.8 mm case. Using \( x\approx k\sqrt{t} \), how long is needed for a 1.2 mm case at the same temperature?
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \dfrac{t_2}{t_1}=\left(\dfrac{x_2}{x_1}\right)^2=\left(\dfrac{1.2}{0.8}\right)^2=1.5^2=2.25 \), so \( t_2=4\times2.25=9 \) hours — more than double the time for 1.5× the depth, because depth grows with the square root of time.

quiz.q:
Why stress relief leaves the microstructure alone

Problem. A welded 0.76% C (eutectoid) bracket is stress-relieved at 650°C for 2 hours. Does this change its pearlite content? Explain using the eutectoid temperature.
quiz.solution: Solution. No. \(650^\circ\text{C} < 727^\circ\text{C}\), the eutectoid temperature, so no austenite ever forms — there is nothing to transform back into pearlite on cooling. The hold only lets locked-in residual stress relax; the 100% pearlite microstructure from welding is unchanged.

quiz.q: Which cooling path gives the SOFTER steel, and why?
choices: Full anneal — slow furnace cooling gives coarse pearlite
Normalize — air cooling gives coarse pearlite
Full anneal — furnace cooling gives fine pearlite
quiz.solution: Slow furnace cooling gives carbon more time to diffuse, producing coarse, widely-spaced — and softer — pearlite.

quiz.q: Stress relief is held below the eutectoid temperature primarily to:
choices: Remove residual stress without re-forming austenite
quiz.solution: Staying below 727°C means no austenite forms — the treatment relaxes stress, not microstructure.

quiz.q: Spheroidizing is used mainly to:
choices: Maximize machinability and formability before heavy cold work
quiz.solution: Spheroidized cementite in a soft ferrite matrix is the most formable condition a high-carbon steel reaches.

quiz.q: Carburizing hardens a low-carbon steel's surface by:
choices: Diffusing carbon into the surface at high temperature, then quenching
Heating the whole part above the eutectoid and furnace-cooling
Heating only the surface with an induction coil
Adding nitrogen at low temperature with no quench
quiz.solution: Carburizing enriches the surface with carbon by diffusion; the quench then hardens that enriched layer.

quiz.q: Nitriding is often preferred over carburizing for finished precision parts because:
choices: It runs at lower temperature with no post-treatment quench, so distortion is minimal
It produces a far deeper case than carburizing
It requires no diffusion step at all
It only works on high-carbon steel
quiz.solution: No quench after nitriding means the already-finished dimensions are not disturbed.

quiz.q: Induction hardening differs from carburizing chiefly because it:
choices: Changes no composition — it heats and quenches only the existing surface layer
Adds carbon to a low-carbon core
Requires hours at high temperature in a furnace atmosphere
Cannot be used on shafts or gears
quiz.solution: Induction hardening relies on rapid local heating and quenching, not diffusion of any element.

quiz.q: In shop sequencing, case hardening is normally scheduled:
choices: After rough machining but before finish grinding, since it distorts the part slightly
After finish grinding, so grinding removes the hardened case
quiz.solution: Rough-machine soft → case-harden → finish-grind to final size keeps the hardened surface intact and on-dimension.

quiz.q: A gear tooth needing a wear-resistant surface AND a fracture-resistant core calls for:
choices: Case hardening — a hard shell over an unhardened, ductile core
quiz.solution: Through-hardening would win the surface and lose the core to brittleness — case hardening keeps both.

3.kuwait:
A shaft drawing that calls out "normalize, then induction-harden the bearing journals to 0.8 mm effective case depth" is specifying exactly the sequence above: soft enough to machine cleanly, then a hard, wear-resistant surface only where a bearing rides, over a core still tough enough to carry shock load. A regional fabrication or machine shop reads that callout, sequences rough machining before hardening and finish grinding after, and treats the case-depth number as a real acceptance criterion, checked on a cross-sectioned sample.

Failure analysis runs the same logic backward. A shaft that snapped rather than wore out, with a glassy, brittle fracture right at the case-core boundary, points to a case pushed too deep or a core hardened along with it — through-hardening dressed up as case hardening. A journal that wore through quickly points to too shallow a case, or a stress-relief step skipped before final grinding, leaving residual stress to help the wear along. Reading the failure back against the treatments above usually tells a maintenance engineer which step in the sequence was missed.

4.lecture:
§1 · Reading the designation

The AISI/SAE four-digit system encodes composition directly. The first two digits name the alloy family — 10xx is plain carbon, 41xx is chromium-molybdenum, 43xx is nickel-chromium-molybdenum, and so on. The last two digits give the carbon content in hundredths of a percent: 4140 is a Cr-Mo steel at \(0.40\)% C; 1020 is plain carbon at \(0.20\)% C. Reading a designation is reading two separate questions at once — which alloying family, and how much carbon — and the two answer different things about the steel entirely.

§2 · What carbon buys, and what alloying buys

Carbon sets the ceiling: the maximum hardness any steel of that composition can reach, achieved right at a quenched surface where cooling is fastest. A 1040 and a 4140 — both \(0.40\)% C — quench to essentially the same peak hardness. What differs is how far that hardness extends inward. Alloying elements — manganese, chromium, molybdenum, nickel — slow the austenite-to-pearlite transformation, so a slower-cooling interior still has time to form the hard, non-equilibrium structure instead of soft pearlite. This property — how deep the hardening penetrates, independent of the peak value — is hardenability, and it is what alloying buys.

§3 · The Jominy end-quench test

Hardenability is measured directly: a standard round bar is austenitized, then one end is water-quenched while the rest air-cools at a rate that falls off smoothly with distance from that end. Hardness measured along the bar's length — the Jominy curve — is a direct picture of hardenability. A shallow-hardenability steel (plain carbon) drops from peak hardness within a few millimetres of the quenched end; a deep-hardenability steel (alloyed) stays near-peak for tens of millimetres. At the classic reference distance of \(12.5\ \text{mm}\) (\(\tfrac{1}{2}\ \text{in}\)), a 1040-type curve has already fallen to the mid-20s HRC while a 4140-type curve is still in the low-to-mid 40s — the same numbers a heat-treater reads off a real Jominy chart to judge whether a given steel will through-harden a given section size.

§4 · Selecting a grade — the working method

Selection runs from the part outward. First, the section size: a thin part cools fast everywhere and a plain-carbon steel may through-harden it fine; a thick shaft's core cools slowly no matter what quenchant is used, and only a deep-hardenability alloy steel will harden there. Second, the service demand: a shaft under bending and torsion needs toughness through its cross-section, which plain-carbon steel — hard skin, soft, weak core — cannot give at any real diameter. Third, cost: alloying elements and the slower, more controlled quenches deep-hardenability grades often need both add cost, so a plain-carbon grade is the right answer whenever section size and service load allow it. A 4140 shaft and a 1040 shaft can leave the quench at the identical surface hardness; only the Jominy curve — and the part's actual thickness — says which one is lying about its core.

Key distinction

Carbon content sets peak hardness. Alloy content sets how far that hardness reaches — hardenability, read directly off the Jominy curve.

Decoding a designation

Problem. A drawing calls for AISI 4340 for a heavy-duty shaft. What does each pair of digits tell you?
quiz.solution: Solution. "43" identifies a nickel-chromium-molybdenum alloy family (high hardenability); "40" means \(0.40\)% carbon, setting the peak achievable hardness — the same peak a plain 1040 bar would reach at its surface, but 4340 holds that hardness far deeper into a thick section.

quiz.q:
Reading the Jominy chart

Problem. At 12.5 mm from the quenched end, a plain-carbon curve reads about 24 HRC and an alloy curve reads about 44 HRC, though both started near 55 HRC at the end. Explain why the two curves start together and end apart.
quiz.solution: Solution. They start together because carbon content — identical in both — sets the peak hardness available right at the fast-quenched end. They end apart because the alloying elements in the second steel slow the pearlite transformation, so even the more slowly-cooled material 12.5 mm in still forms a hard structure instead of soft pearlite: greater hardenability, same carbon ceiling.

quiz.q:
Choosing a grade for section size

Problem. A 100 mm diameter shaft needs to be hard and tough through its full cross-section. A 1040 bar and a 4140 bar reach identical surface hardness when quenched. Which grade should be specified, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. 4140. At 100 mm diameter the core cools far too slowly for the plain-carbon 1040 to do anything but form soft pearlite there — its hardenability does not reach the center. 4140's alloying content extends hardening deep enough to still harden that core, even though both grades would look identical at the surface alone.

quiz.q: The last two digits of a steel designation like 4140 give:
choices: Carbon content in hundredths of a percent
quiz.solution: xx40 means 0.40% carbon; the first two digits name the alloy family instead.

quiz.q: Peak achievable hardness at a quenched surface is set mainly by:
quiz.solution: Carbon sets the hardness ceiling; alloying decides how deep that ceiling is reached.

quiz.q: Hardenability is best defined as:
choices: How deep hardening penetrates from the quenched surface
The maximum hardness a steel can reach
How hard a steel is at room temperature before treatment
The melting point of the alloy
quiz.solution: Two steels can share a peak hardness and differ completely in hardenability — the depth that hardness reaches.

quiz.q: In the Jominy end-quench test, hardness is measured:
choices: Along the bar's length, moving away from the quenched end
Only at the far, air-cooled end
quiz.solution: The full curve — hardness at every distance from the quenched end — is the point of the test.

quiz.q: Alloying elements such as Cr, Mo, and Ni raise hardenability mainly by:
quiz.solution: A slower pearlite transformation gives more of the cross-section time to form a hard structure instead.

quiz.q: A thin part that cools quickly everywhere is often specified in plain-carbon steel rather than an alloy grade because:
choices: Fast cooling reaches every section, so extra hardenability isn't needed
quiz.solution: When the whole section already cools fast, the alloy grade's main advantage — depth — goes unused; plain carbon does the job for less.

quiz.q: Two steels reach identical surface hardness after quenching. This alone tells you:
choices: They likely share the same carbon content, not necessarily the same hardenability
quiz.solution: Surface hardness reflects carbon content; only the depth profile (the Jominy curve) reveals hardenability.

quiz.q: In a designation like 4140, which pair of digits identifies the alloy family (which elements besides carbon are present)?
choices: Neither — alloy family is stamped separately
quiz.solution: First two digits = alloy family (here, chromium-molybdenum); last two = carbon content.

4.kuwait:
A procurement request that specifies "4140" instead of "1040" for a heavy shaft is not asking for a harder steel at the surface — a heat-treater can get both to nearly the same surface HRC. It is asking for a steel that stays hard deep enough into a thick section to carry load without a soft, fatigue-prone core. Reading a Jominy curve, or even just recognizing the alloy-family digits on a mill certificate, is how a maintenance or design engineer checks that the grade delivered actually matches the section size it has to survive in.

Substitution decisions run into this constantly: a store only has 1045 in stock where the drawing calls for 4140. For a small-diameter pin the substitution may be harmless — both harden through at that size. For a large shaft it is not, however similar the two grades look on a hardness tester at the surface; the core of the 1045 shaft will stay soft. The Jominy curve, not the spec sheet's single hardness number, is what settles whether a substitution is safe.

5.lecture:
§1 · The stainless families

Any steel with roughly \(10.5\)–\(12\)% chromium or more forms a thin, adherent, self-healing chromium-oxide film — passivity — and stops rusting the way plain carbon steel does. Above that composition floor, four structural families cover almost every application. Austenitic stainless (the 300 series: Cr + Ni, FCC, non-magnetic) gives the best combination of corrosion resistance, ductility, and weldability, but cannot be hardened by heat treatment — only by cold work. Ferritic stainless (Cr only, BCC, magnetic) is cheaper and adequate where corrosion demands are moderate. Martensitic stainless carries enough carbon to quench-harden exactly like a plain carbon steel — traded corrosion resistance for the ability to reach real hardness, used for cutlery, shafts, and valve trim. Duplex stainless is a roughly 50/50 austenite–ferrite structure, combining ferritic-level strength with corrosion resistance that rivals or beats austenitic grades in chloride-rich environments.

§2 · Sensitization — reading the diagram

Welding heats the metal beside the weld — the heat-affected zone — through the sensitizing range, roughly \(450\)–\(850^\circ\)C, for long enough that chromium carbides (\(\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6\)) precipitate directly onto grain boundaries. Forming those carbides pulls chromium out of the matrix immediately next to the boundary — not from the bulk, which stays fully alloyed, but from a narrow band exactly where the carbides sit. If that local concentration drops below the passivity threshold, the boundary loses its protective film while the surrounding grains keep theirs, and corrosion attacks precisely along the grain boundaries — intergranular corrosion, sometimes called weld decay. The working method: find the heat-affected zone, confirm it passed through the sensitizing range, and treat every grain boundary there as the weak point, not the plate as a whole.

Three fixes address it directly: low-carbon "L" grades (304L, 316L, \(C<0.03\)%) simply have too little carbon to form much \(\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6\); stabilized grades (321, 347) add titanium or niobium, which forms TiC or NbC preferentially and leaves the chromium in solution; and a post-weld solution anneal (reheat above \(\sim\!1050^\circ\)C, then quench) redissolves any carbides that did form and re-homogenizes the chromium before the part enters service.

§3 · Cast irons — one base metal, three graphite shapes

Cast iron is iron with \(2\)–\(4\)% carbon, and its mechanical character comes almost entirely from what shape the excess carbon takes as graphite. Gray iron lets graphite grow as sharp, interconnected flakes; each flake is a built-in crack, so gray iron is brittle with near-zero elongation — but it is cheap, castable, an excellent vibration damper, and strong in compression, which is exactly what a machine base or engine block needs. Ductile (nodular) iron adds a trace of magnesium or cerium before pouring, which forces the graphite into spheroids instead of flakes; spheroids concentrate far less stress, so ductile iron reaches real tensile strength and real elongation (typically \(10\)–\(18\)%) — strong enough for crankshafts, gears, and pressure-piping fittings. White iron is cooled fast enough (or alloyed) that the carbon stays locked as cementite and never forms graphite at all — extremely hard, extremely brittle, used only where pure abrasive wear resistance matters and impact loading does not, such as mill liners and crusher jaws.

§4 · Selecting a family — the working method

For stainless: default to austenitic unless a specific constraint rules it out — need to heat-treat-harden the part, go martensitic; need maximum strength with strong chloride resistance, go duplex; need to hold cost down where corrosion demands are mild, ferritic is enough. For cast iron: a non-critical, compressively-loaded, vibration-damped part is gray iron's job; anything that must also survive tension or shock — a crankshaft, a gear, a pipe fitting — needs ductile iron instead, even though it costs more than gray; a purely abrasive, low-impact wear surface is the one place white iron's brittleness is an acceptable price for its hardness. Both decisions run the same way: name the dominant load the part actually sees, then pick the family built for that load, not the cheapest one that merely looks similar on a datasheet.

Key mechanism — sensitization

Welding drives the HAZ through 450–850°C long enough for Cr₂₃C₆ to precipitate at grain boundaries, pulling local Cr below the ~12% passivity floor right at the boundary — while the bulk alloy is untouched.

  • Grain boundaries (Materials I) — where sensitization happens.
  • Corrosion mechanisms return in more depth in Lesson 10 and in Year 3's full corrosion course — this lesson previews only what welding does to a grain boundary.

quiz.q:
Is the heat-affected zone still passive?

Problem. A 304 stainless weld\'s heat-affected zone measures \(9\)% Cr immediately next to a grain boundary; the bulk plate is \(18\)% Cr. Is that boundary region protected by a passive film? What repair grade avoids this?
quiz.solution: Solution. No — \(9\)% is below the \(\sim\!12\)% passivity threshold, so that narrow band is unprotected even though the bulk plate is fine. Re-welding in 304L (low-carbon) or a stabilized grade like 321/347 avoids re-forming enough \(\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6\) to deplete the boundary again.

quiz.q:
Reading a fracture back to the alloy

Problem. A crankshaft snapped in service. The fracture surface is dull gray, and measured elongation at failure was near \(0\)%. Ductile iron of similar composition normally shows \(10\)–\(18\)% elongation. Which iron was this, and was it the right choice for a crankshaft?
quiz.solution: Solution. The dull-gray, near-zero-elongation fracture is gray iron's signature — its graphite flakes give it essentially no tensile ductility. It was the wrong choice: a crankshaft carries cyclic bending and torsional shock, exactly the load gray iron's flake graphite cannot survive. Ductile (nodular) iron, or forged steel, is the correct specification.

quiz.q:
Choosing a stainless family for seawater service

Problem. A chloride-laden seawater cooling line needs a stainless pipe with both high strength (to reduce wall thickness) and strong resistance to chloride pitting. Which family fits, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. Duplex. Its mixed austenite-ferrite structure gives it higher strength than austenitic grades (thinner walls for the same pressure rating) while matching or beating austenitic chloride-pitting resistance — the combination plain austenitic or ferritic grades cannot offer alone.

quiz.q: A stainless steel's passive film is formed by:
choices: Chromium forming a thin, self-healing chromium-oxide layer
quiz.solution: Chromium above ~10.5–12% forms the adherent oxide film that makes the steel "stainless."

quiz.q: Austenitic stainless steel (e.g. 304, 316) is best identified by:
choices: FCC structure, non-magnetic, Cr + Ni alloyed
Highest carbon content of the stainless families
quiz.solution: Austenitic grades are FCC and non-magnetic — the family with Ni added alongside Cr.

quiz.q: Which stainless family can be hardened by quenching, like a plain-carbon steel?
quiz.solution: Martensitic stainless carries enough carbon to quench-harden, trading some corrosion resistance for hardness.

quiz.q: Duplex stainless is chosen over plain austenitic grades mainly because it offers:
choices: Higher strength with chloride-pitting resistance that matches or beats austenitic grades
Lower cost with no corrosion resistance
Full heat-treat hardenability like a martensitic grade
Better performance only in freshwater, never chlorides
quiz.solution: The mixed austenite-ferrite structure gives duplex grades both higher strength and strong chloride resistance at once.

quiz.q: Sensitization happens when:
choices: Cr₂₃C₆ precipitates at grain boundaries during welding, depleting nearby Cr
The entire part loses all of its chromium
It only ever affects ferritic stainless
The steel is left at room temperature too long
quiz.solution: Carbide precipitation at boundaries — not bulk chromium loss — is the mechanism.

quiz.q: Low-carbon "L" grades (304L, 316L) resist sensitization mainly because:
choices: Less carbon means far less Cr₂₃C₆ forms, leaving more Cr in solution
They contain no chromium to deplete
They form carbides faster, using up the carbon safely
quiz.solution: Starving the carbide-forming reaction of carbon is the direct fix.

quiz.q: Gray iron's low tensile ductility comes from:
choices: Graphite flakes acting as built-in stress risers
Having more iron than ductile iron
Never being fully melted during casting
quiz.solution: Sharp interconnected flakes concentrate stress and initiate cracks early; ductile iron avoids this with nodules instead.

quiz.q: Ductile (nodular) iron gets its toughness from:
choices: A small Mg or Ce addition that makes graphite form as rounded nodules
Fast cooling that forms cementite instead of graphite
quiz.solution: Nodules, unlike flakes, do not concentrate stress nearly as badly — real elongation follows.

5.kuwait:
A coastal process plant's seawater cooling piping is a standing materials-selection problem: chlorides attack austenitic stainless by pitting given enough time, which is why duplex grades increasingly win that specification despite costing more per meter — the thinner wall duplex allows can offset some of the premium. Field-welded repairs on any stainless line raise the sensitization question immediately: a repair welded in standard 316 without an L-grade or a post-weld anneal leaves every grain boundary in the heat-affected zone exposed to exactly the intergranular attack this lesson describes, often invisible until the pipe leaks along a ring around the weld.

Pump casings and valve bodies across a plant are commonly gray or ductile iron, and a cracked casing pulled during a shutdown is read the same way a fracture is read in the lab: a dull, flat, no-warning break is gray iron behaving exactly as gray iron does under a load it was never rated for; a break showing visible deformation before final fracture points to ductile iron that failed from a real overload or a missed defect, not from the material being the wrong choice.

6.lecture:
§1 · Aluminum and its temper system

Wrought aluminum alloys are named by a four-digit code whose first digit gives the principal alloying element — 2xxx is Al-Cu, 5xxx is Al-Mg, 6xxx is Al-Mg-Si, 7xxx is Al-Zn — the same logic as a steel designation's family digits, applied to a different metal. A temper suffix then says what was done to it: F is as-fabricated, O is fully annealed (softest), H covers strain-hardened tempers used on alloys that cannot be heat-treated to strengthen, and T covers thermally treated tempers. Of the T-series, T4 means solution heat treated and naturally aged at room temperature over days to weeks; T6 means solution heat treated and artificially aged at a moderate elevated temperature to reach peak strength in hours.

§2 · Reading the age-hardening curve

Solution treatment heats an Al-Cu alloy hot enough to dissolve its copper fully into solid solution, then quenches fast enough to trap that copper in a supersaturated, still-soft state. Aging — holding at a moderate temperature — lets that trapped copper begin precipitating as extremely fine particles distributed through the matrix; those fine particles block dislocation motion far more effectively than the same copper sitting dissolved in solution, so strength climbs as aging proceeds. Underaged material simply hasn't aged long enough — precipitates are still too small and sparse. At the peak (T6), precipitate size and spacing are exactly tuned to obstruct dislocations most effectively. Held longer still, the precipitates keep growing — overaging — becoming fewer, larger, and more widely spaced, which dislocations can bypass more easily again, so strength falls even though the alloy spent more time at temperature, not less.

§3 · Copper and nickel alloys

Copper's defining property is conductivity, and its central engineering trade-off follows directly: anything that strengthens copper — alloying or cold work — also degrades that conductivity. Conductor-grade copper is drawn through a series of dies to final wire size, and each pass work-hardens it, piling up dislocations and using up the ductility needed for the next pass; drawn far enough without relief, it cracks instead of continuing to draw down. An interpass anneal — a recrystallization heat treatment — restores ductility between passes so drawing can continue. Brass (Cu-Zn) is cheaper and more machinable; bronze (Cu-Sn or Cu-Al) is tougher and more wear- and corrosion-resistant; both are prized in hazardous atmospheres for being non-sparking. Nickel alloys hold their strength at temperatures where steel and aluminum have both lost most of theirs, and resist corrosion in chemical environments that attack stainless steel outright — earning their considerable cost premium only where nothing cheaper survives.

§4 · Choosing a nonferrous family — the working method

Where weight and moderate strength matter most, aluminum wins — then the temper is chosen for the job: T6 for a structural part, O or an H-temper for sheet that still needs to be formed afterward. Where electrical or thermal conductivity is the actual requirement, copper is the answer, and the specification states plainly whether annealed (soft, most conductive, most formable) or hard-drawn (stronger, still adequately conductive) is needed. Where the service temperature or the chemical environment would defeat steel or stainless outright, a nickel alloy is the only real option, and its cost is simply the price of the part surviving at all. Each family earns its premium over plain steel by solving one specific problem steel cannot — the working method is naming that problem before naming the alloy.

Key curve — age hardening

Strength rises as fine, coherent precipitates form and block dislocations (underaged → peak, T6) — then FALLS as those same precipitates coarsen (overaged). More time at temperature is not always better.

What this lesson assumes

  • Dislocations and strengthening mechanisms (Materials I) — precipitates block dislocation motion the same way grain boundaries and solute atoms do.
  • Quenching and supersaturation (Lesson 2) — the same "trap a non-equilibrium state, then let it evolve" idea used for steel's martensite.
  • Work hardening (Materials I) — why copper needs interpass annealing.

quiz.q:
Reading the aging curve

Problem. An Al-Cu alloy ages from \(150\ \text{MPa}\) (solution-treated) toward a T6 peak of \(420\ \text{MPa}\) at aging time \(t=100\). At \(t=40\) it measures about \(347\ \text{MPa}\); at \(t=250\) it measures about \(301\ \text{MPa}\). Is the \(t=250\) part underaged, peak-aged, or overaged — and what should change about its aging cycle?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(t=250\) is far past the peak at \(t=100\), so it is overaged — and its \(301\ \text{MPa}\) is even below the \(t=40\) underaged value of \(347\ \text{MPa}\). The aging cycle should be shortened, stopping near \(t=100\), not extended: more time at temperature made this part weaker, not stronger.

quiz.q:
Why the copper wire cracked

Problem. A copper wire is cold-drawn through several dies with no anneal step between passes and begins cracking on the final pass. What metallurgical process caused this, and what single step would have prevented it?
quiz.solution: Solution. Work hardening: each pass piles up more dislocations and uses up the ductility available for the next draw. An interpass anneal — a recrystallization heat treatment restoring soft, ductile grains — between passes would have let drawing continue without cracking.

quiz.q:
Family selection for a 650°C bracket

Problem. A turbine hot-section bracket must survive continuous service at \(650^\circ\)C, a temperature at which both aluminum and most steels have lost the bulk of their strength. Which alloy family is appropriate, and what is the trade-off?
quiz.solution: Solution. A nickel alloy (superalloy) — it retains useful strength at temperatures that defeat steel and aluminum outright. The trade-off is cost: nickel alloys carry a significant premium, justified only because no cheaper family survives that service temperature.

quiz.q: In an aluminum temper code, "T6" means:
choices: Solution heat treated and artificially aged to peak strength
quiz.solution: T6 is the peak-strength artificially-aged temper.

quiz.q: On the age-hardening curve, strength eventually falls with continued aging time because:
choices: Precipitates coarsen — fewer, larger, more widely spaced — making dislocation motion easier again
Aging always stops working after a fixed time regardless of temperature
quiz.solution: Overaging coarsens the precipitates rather than removing them, and dislocations move around larger, sparser particles more easily.

quiz.q: "Underaged" aluminum has:
choices: Strength below the T6 peak because precipitates haven't finished forming
quiz.solution: Underaged simply means aging stopped before reaching the strength peak.

quiz.q: Copper conductor drawn through several dies with no interpass anneal will:
quiz.solution: Cold work piles up dislocations pass after pass until ductility runs out.

quiz.q: The main engineering trade-off in strengthening copper (by alloying or cold work) is:
choices: Strength rises while electrical conductivity falls
Strength rises with no other cost
Copper cannot be strengthened at all
quiz.solution: Whatever raises copper's strength also degrades the conductivity that is its main reason for use.

quiz.q: Nickel alloys are specified over steel mainly when:
choices: The application needs strength retention at very high temperature or resistance in a very aggressive chemical environment
The part must be as light as possible
Electrical conductivity is the main requirement
quiz.solution: Nickel alloys earn their premium only where steel or stainless would fail outright.

quiz.q: In an aluminum designation such as 6061, the family digit ("6") mainly indicates:
choices: The year the alloy was introduced
quiz.solution: The first digit names the alloy family by its principal alloying addition; temper is a separate suffix (O, H, T…).

quiz.q: An overaged T6-family part, compared to its own peak-aged state, is:
choices: Weaker, because its precipitates have coarsened past the optimum size and spacing
Stronger, because it spent more time at temperature
Identical — aging time beyond T6 has no effect
quiz.solution: Coarsened precipitates are easier for dislocations to bypass, so measured strength drops below the T6 peak.

6.kuwait:
A structural bracket specified as 6061-T6 versus the same shape supplied as 6061-O is the temper system doing real engineering work: the O-temper sheet forms easily into shape without cracking, then a solution-and-age cycle afterward brings it up to T6 strength for service — ordering the wrong temper for the stage of fabrication is a common, avoidable mistake on a shop floor. Copper busbar and cable runs in a plant's electrical distribution show the conductivity trade-off directly: hard-drawn copper is used where mechanical strength in the run matters, annealed (soft) copper where maximum conductivity and flexibility matter more, and the two are not interchangeable even though both are "just copper."

Nickel-alloy components appear only where the budget for anything cheaper has already failed: furnace fixtures, high-temperature process piping, and hardware in aggressive chemical service that would pit or crack a stainless equivalent within a maintenance cycle or two. Recognizing a nickel-alloy part on a bill of materials is usually a signal that an earlier steel or stainless version of that same part failed in service — the specification upgraded because the environment demanded it, not by preference.

7.lecture:
§1 · Fatigue and the S–N curve

Fatigue is failure under repeated, cyclic stress — often at amplitudes far below the material's static strength — and it is the dominant failure mode in any rotating or vibrating part: shafts, bearings, gears, piping under pulsation. The S–N curve plots stress amplitude against cycles to failure on a log scale and is the fundamental fatigue chart. For steel, the curve bends and flattens near \(10^6\)–\(10^7\) cycles into an endurance limit \(S_e\): stay below it and the steel can, in principle, survive indefinitely. A useful first-cut estimate is \( S_e\approx 0.5\times\text{UTS} \) — for a \(800\ \text{MPa}\) UTS steel, roughly \(400\ \text{MPa}\) — but this is a starting point only, always reduced by knockdown factors for surface finish, size, notches, temperature, and required reliability before it is trusted as a design number.

§2 · Fracture mechanics — the simplest honest version

Every real part contains flaws — inclusions, porosity, machining marks, corrosion pits — and fracture mechanics asks a direct question: given a flaw of size \(a\) under applied stress \(\sigma\), will it grow catastrophically? The stress-intensity factor \( K=Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a} \) (with \(Y\), a geometry factor, near \(1\) for simple cases) measures how strongly the crack tip is loaded. Fracture occurs when \(K\) reaches the material's fracture toughness \(K_{IC}\) — a measured, tabulated property. This is why a part can pass a smooth, unflawed tensile test and still fail in service at half that stress: the tensile specimen had no crack to grow, but a flawed in-service part can reach \(K_{IC}\) at a stress far below its own tensile strength. Toughness, not strength alone, sets the real limit once a flaw exists.

§3 · Reading a fracture surface, and creep

A fatigue fracture surface carries two classic tells: beach marks (macroscopic concentric arcs, from crack growth pausing and resuming under varying load) and, under magnification, striations — one per load cycle. Neither appears on a ductile overload fracture, which instead shows necking, shear lips, and a fibrous, dimpled surface; a brittle overload fracture is flat, grainy, with little visible deformation. Reading which of these a fracture surface shows is the fastest diagnostic step in any failure investigation.

Creep is slow, time-dependent permanent deformation under sustained stress at elevated temperature — a practical concern once service temperature exceeds roughly \(0.4\) times the absolute melting temperature (the same homologous-temperature idea from Materials I). It runs in three stages: primary (decelerating strain rate), secondary or steady-state (constant, minimum rate — the regime used for life estimates), and tertiary (accelerating strain rate to rupture). Furnace hardware, turbine blades, and high-temperature piping running hot for years are creep-life designs, not simple strength designs.

§4 · Reading a failure — the working method

Given a failed part: (1) read the fracture surface first — beach marks or striations mean fatigue; necking and dimples mean ductile overload; a flat, grainy surface with no deformation means brittle overload; heavy deformation with intergranular voids means creep. (2) If fatigue, find the initiation site — almost always a surface flaw or stress concentration, since fatigue cracks start where local stress is highest. (3) Compare the operating stress against the S–N curve (was this a foreseeable number of cycles at this amplitude) and, if a flaw is visible, against \(K_{IC}\) (was the flaw large enough to explain failure at this stress). That sequence is exactly how a failure investigation moves from "it broke" to a defensible reason why.

Key rule — first-cut endurance limit

\[ S_e \approx 0.5\times\text{UTS}\quad\text{(steel; before knockdown factors)}. \]

quiz.q:
First-cut endurance limit

Problem. A polished steel has \(\text{UTS}=800\ \text{MPa}\). What first-cut endurance limit does the \(0.5\times\text{UTS}\) rule suggest, before knockdown factors?
quiz.solution: Solution. \( S_e\approx0.5\times800=400\ \text{MPa}\). This is a starting estimate only — surface finish, size, notch, temperature, and reliability knockdown factors would all reduce it before it is used as a design value.

quiz.q:
Does the flaw control failure?

Problem. A pressure-vessel steel has \(\text{UTS}=800\ \text{MPa}\) and \(K_{IC}=100\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}\). Inspection finds a surface flaw \(a=6\ \text{mm}\), geometry factor \(Y\approx1\). Find the fracture stress and say whether the flaw or general yielding controls failure.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \sigma=\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}=\dfrac{100}{\sqrt{\pi\times0.006}}=\dfrac{100}{0.1373}\approx728\ \text{MPa}\). Since \(728\ \text{MPa} < 800\ \text{MPa}\) (UTS), the flaw controls: the vessel fractures at the flaw before it would ever reach general tensile failure.

quiz.q:
Creep life from the steady-state rate

Problem. A turbine blade shows a steady-state (secondary) creep strain rate of \(2\times10^{-6}\) per hour. The design allows a maximum accumulated creep strain of \(1\)% before replacement. Estimate the design life, ignoring the primary and tertiary stages.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( t=\dfrac{0.01}{2\times10^{-6}}=5000\ \text{hours} \). Real designs also budget for the shorter, accelerating tertiary stage as a safety margin, but the steady-state rate gives the working estimate.

quiz.q: The first-cut rule of thumb for a steel's endurance limit is:
quiz.solution: 0.5×UTS is the standard first-cut estimate, always reduced further by real-world knockdown factors.

quiz.q: Which alloys generally have NO true endurance limit?
quiz.solution: Aluminum's S-N curve keeps declining slowly with no flat asymptote, unlike steel's.

quiz.q: Beach marks and striations on a fracture surface indicate:
quiz.solution: These are the two classic macro/micro signatures of cyclic crack growth.

quiz.q: A ductile overload fracture surface typically shows:
choices: Necking and a fibrous, dimpled surface
quiz.solution: Visible plastic deformation and a dimpled, fibrous texture mark a ductile overload break.

quiz.q: The stress-intensity factor K depends on:
choices: Applied stress, flaw size, and geometry
quiz.solution: K = Yσ√(πa) combines all three.

quiz.q: Fracture occurs when:
choices: K reaches the material's fracture toughness K_IC
The part reaches its endurance limit
The part is cyclically loaded even once
quiz.solution: K=K_IC is the fracture criterion in this simplified treatment.

quiz.q: Secondary (steady-state) creep is the design-relevant stage because:
choices: It has a constant, minimum strain rate usable for life estimation
It never actually occurs in real parts
It only matters below room temperature
quiz.solution: Its constant rate is what makes a simple life estimate possible.

quiz.q: Creep becomes a practical concern roughly when:
choices: Service temperature exceeds about 0.4 times the absolute melting temperature
The part is loaded even briefly
The part is made of aluminum specifically
quiz.solution: The homologous-temperature threshold (~0.4 T_m) is the standard rule of thumb for when creep matters.

7.kuwait:
Rotating equipment — pump shafts, compressor rotors, fan blades — lives or dies by the S–N curve: a shaft sized to keep vibration-induced stress amplitude under its endurance limit can run for years, while the same shaft nudged above that line by a growing misalignment or an unbalanced load is now accumulating fatigue damage on every single rotation, invisibly, until it snaps. A shutdown investigation into a broken shaft starts exactly at §3: beach marks radiating from a keyway or fillet almost always mean fatigue from a stress concentration the design underestimated, not a one-time overload.

High-temperature plant hardware — furnace rolls, superheater tubing, anything running hot under sustained load for years — is a creep problem as much as a strength problem. A component pulled during a turnaround showing measurable permanent elongation, well before any fracture, is exhibiting secondary creep exactly as described above; tracking that accumulated strain against the 1% (or whatever the design allows) is how a reliability engineer decides whether the component gets one more run or gets replaced now.

8.lecture:
§1 · Thermoplastics versus thermosets

Thermoplastics are chains — linear or branched — held together only by weak secondary (van der Waals, entanglement) forces between neighboring chains. Heat overcomes those weak forces, so a thermoplastic softens and melts, and can be re-melted and reshaped repeatedly: PE, PP, PVC, nylon, and PTFE all work this way. Thermosets instead cure through an irreversible chemical reaction that links chains together with strong covalent bonds into a single connected network. Heating a cured thermoset does not remelt it — there is no weak bond left to overcome — it chars or decomposes at high enough temperature instead. Epoxy, phenolic resin, and cured polyurethane are thermosets; vulcanized rubber behaves the same way, since vulcanization crosslinks its chains exactly as curing does.

§2 · The glass transition — reading the diagram

Below the glass transition temperature \(T_g\), an amorphous polymer's chain segments are essentially frozen — too little thermal energy for them to rotate or slide past one another — and the material is glassy: stiff, and often brittle. Above \(T_g\), segments gain enough mobility to move, and modulus falls dramatically, often two to three orders of magnitude over a narrow temperature range. A crosslinked elastomer levels off above \(T_g\) at a low rubbery plateau — the crosslinks stop it from simply flowing away — and that rubbery, resilient state is exactly what makes an elastomer useful as a seal: it can be compressed and spring back. An uncrosslinked thermoplastic above its own \(T_g\) has no such plateau; it keeps softening toward viscous flow instead.

§3 · Elastomers for seals — the O-ring, and the Gulf's real risk

An O-ring seals by staying elastically compressed between two mating surfaces, which only works while it remains in its rubbery state. Drop it below \(T_g\) and it goes glassy and stiff, loses its ability to spring back into the groove, and leaks — a cold-start O-ring failure is one of the most famous documented failure modes in engineering history, from a well-known spacecraft accident where an unusually cold morning stiffened the seals past their working range. In Gulf-region service, ambient and process heat sit well above almost any common elastomer's \(T_g\), so cold-embrittlement is rarely the practical risk — except where the equipment itself runs cold regardless of climate: refrigeration lines, cryogenic or LNG-adjacent service, chilled process streams. The diagram's other landmark, at the high-temperature end, is the one that actually bites in sustained Gulf heat: a practical service-temperature ceiling set by thermal and oxidative degradation — chain scission, hardening, cracking — a completely different mechanism from \(T_g\), reached from the opposite direction. A seal compound must also resist the specific fluid it contacts; one rated for water may swell or soften in a hydrocarbon it was never qualified for.

§4 · Selecting a polymer or elastomer — the working method

Given a service temperature window and a chemical environment: (1) confirm the low end sits safely above the candidate's \(T_g\), with real margin, not right at the line; (2) confirm the high end stays below its practical thermal/oxidative service limit; (3) confirm chemical compatibility with the actual process fluid, not "elastomer" as a generic category; (4) choose thermoplastic (needs to be formed or re-formed) versus a crosslinked thermoset or elastomer (needs to flex and recover its shape). Skipping any one of these four checks is how the wrong seal compound ends up in service.

Key states

Below \(T_g\): glassy, stiff, brittle. Through \(T_g\): modulus falls 2–3 orders of magnitude. Above \(T_g\) (crosslinked): rubbery plateau — the state a seal must stay in to function.

  • Modulus as a stiffness measure (Materials I).
  • Reading a property-vs-temperature diagram, as in Lessons 1–7.

quiz.q:
Will the O-ring seal?

Problem. An O-ring compound has \(T_g=-20^\circ\)C (representative). A refrigerant line it seals runs at \(-30^\circ\)C during startup. Will it seal reliably at that moment? What about the same line at \(40^\circ\)C Gulf ambient?
quiz.solution: Solution. At \(-30^\circ\)C, the compound is below its \(-20^\circ\)C \(T_g\) — glassy and stiff, so it will not seal reliably. At \(40^\circ\)C, well above \(T_g\), it is in its rubbery working state and seals fine, assuming that temperature is also below its high-temperature service ceiling.

quiz.q:
Same fire, two different polymers

Problem. A cured epoxy housing and a polyethylene (thermoplastic) housing of similar size are both heated to \(300^\circ\)C in a fire. Describe what happens to each, and why they differ.
quiz.solution: Solution. Polyethylene melts and flows well before \(300^\circ\)C (its melting point is roughly \(130\)–\(140^\circ\)C) because heat alone overcomes the weak forces between its chains. The epoxy will not melt at any temperature — its covalent crosslink network can only be undone by chemical decomposition, not simple heating — so it chars and degrades in place while roughly holding its shape until decomposition proceeds far enough.

quiz.q:
Reading the modulus drop

Problem. Reading the diagram, modulus falls from about \(1000\ \text{MPa}\) glassy to about \(2\ \text{MPa}\) in the rubbery plateau. Express this drop as a ratio and in orders of magnitude.
quiz.solution: Solution. Ratio \( = 1000/2 = 500\). \( \log_{10}(500)\approx2.7 \), so the modulus falls by roughly \(2.5\)–\(3\) orders of magnitude through the transition — consistent with the "two to three orders of magnitude" describing the glass transition generally.

quiz.q: Thermoplastics can be re-melted and reshaped because:
choices: Their chains are held only by weak secondary forces, not covalent crosslinks
Heat has no effect on their structure
quiz.solution: Weak inter-chain forces, not a permanent network, is what allows repeated melting.

quiz.q: Thermosets do NOT re-melt when heated because:
choices: Curing forms a permanent covalent crosslink network
They are always liquid at room temperature
quiz.solution: A covalent network cannot be undone by heat alone — only chemical decomposition breaks it.

quiz.q: Below its glass transition temperature, an amorphous polymer is:
quiz.solution: Frozen chain segments below Tg give the glassy, stiff, brittle state.

quiz.q: Through the glass transition, a polymer's modulus typically:
choices: Falls by roughly 2–3 orders of magnitude over a narrow temperature range
quiz.solution: The dramatic modulus drop through Tg is the defining feature of the transition.

quiz.q: An O-ring must stay in its ___ state to seal reliably:
quiz.solution: Only the rubbery, resilient state lets an O-ring stay compressed and spring back.

quiz.q: In a hot Gulf process environment, an elastomer seal's more common practical failure mode is:
choices: Thermal/oxidative degradation at the high-temperature end, not Tg embrittlement
Tg embrittlement is always the dominant Gulf risk
quiz.solution: Gulf ambient sits well above Tg for most compounds; the high-temperature ceiling is the mechanism that actually bites.

quiz.q: Selecting a seal compound requires checking chemical compatibility with:
choices: The actual process fluid it will contact
Only its manufacturer's country of origin
quiz.solution: A compound rated for one fluid can swell or degrade in another it was never qualified against.

quiz.q: Vulcanized rubber behaves like a thermoset because:
choices: Vulcanization crosslinks its chains, similar to curing
It has no chains at all
It contains no sulfur or crosslinking agent
quiz.solution: Vulcanization builds the same kind of permanent crosslink network that curing builds in a thermoset.

8.kuwait:
Gasket and O-ring specification on a process plant is a §4 checklist applied line by line: a seal qualified for one process stream is not automatically safe in another, and the two failure directions are genuinely different — a chilled or refrigerant line risks the cold, glassy side of the diagram, while the great majority of hot process piping and static seals risk the thermal-degradation side, hardening and cracking after months in service rather than failing suddenly on a cold morning. Reading a field-returned O-ring that has gone hard and cracked, versus one that simply lost its spring-back, points a maintenance engineer toward which end of the diagram actually failed.

Housings and covers on the same plant are a live thermoplastic-versus-thermoset decision: a thermoplastic enclosure can be repaired by local heating and reshaping in a pinch, while a cracked thermoset housing cannot be re-melted back into shape by any amount of heat — it can only be replaced. Knowing which family a given part is, before reaching for a heat gun, is the difference between a five-minute field fix and a part destroyed by the attempt.

9.lecture:
§1 · Why ceramics fail statistically

Ceramics are strong in compression but weak and unreliable in tension, and the reason is the same in both cases: strength is controlled by the largest flaw — a micro-crack or pore — present in the stressed volume, not by an intrinsic bulk property the way a ductile metal's yield strength is. Every sample carries a different largest flaw, placed randomly by the manufacturing process, so identical samples tested identically fail at different stresses — strength is a distribution, not a single number. Under tension, a flaw concentrates stress at its tip and, with essentially no ductility available to blunt or redistribute that stress, can grow catastrophically; the same flaw under compression tends to close rather than open, which is why "tension is the enemy" for any ceramic component.

§2 · The Weibull distribution — reading the diagram

The probability that a sample fails at or below stress \(\sigma\) follows the Weibull distribution, \( P_f(\sigma)=1-\exp\!\left[-(\sigma/\sigma_0)^m\right] \). \(\sigma_0\), the characteristic strength, is the stress at which about \(63\)% of samples are expected to have failed. \(m\), the Weibull modulus, describes the spread: a high \(m\) gives a narrow, predictable strength distribution (a well-controlled process); a low \(m\) gives a wide, unpredictable one, where some samples fail at far lower stress than others. Reading the curve directly answers why ceramic strength is statistical: at \(\sigma=250\ \text{MPa}\), roughly \(21\)% of identical samples are already expected to have failed; raise the stress by a modest \(40\)% to \(350\ \text{MPa}\) and failure probability jumps to about \(97\)%. That is an extremely narrow, unforgiving safe-operating window compared to a ductile metal — which is why ceramic components carry large safety margins and are often proof-tested: loaded once to a screening stress before service, specifically to break off the weak tail of the distribution before it can fail in the field.

§3 · Refractories and fiber composites

Refractories are ceramics engineered specifically to survive sustained high temperature — furnace, kiln, and ladle linings — without melting, softening, or reacting with what they contain. A high melting point alone is not enough: the lining must also have low thermal conductivity (to insulate the steel shell behind it) and chemical compatibility with the slag or metal it contacts, since an incompatible refractory can be chemically attacked and dissolved over time well below its own melting point. Fiber composites pair a strong, stiff fiber (glass, carbon, aramid) that carries the load with a matrix — usually polymer — that holds the fibers in position, transfers load between them, and protects them from the environment. Neither material alone reaches the composite's combination of stiffness, strength, and low weight; industrial hardware built this way includes fiberglass piping and tanks in corrosive service and carbon-fiber components wherever weight is a genuine constraint.

§4 · Working with ceramics and composites — the working method

For ceramics: design for compression wherever the geometry allows it, keep tensile stresses low with generous safety factors, avoid sharp corners or point contacts that locally raise tension, and proof-test when reliability truly matters. For composites: identify the direction the load actually travels — a composite is strongly directional, strong along the fiber and comparatively weak across it, unlike an isotropic metal — and orient plies to carry that load; then treat the matrix's temperature and chemical limits, usually lower than the fiber's own, as the real service ceiling for the whole part.

Key tool — Weibull strength distribution

\[ P_f(\sigma)=1-\exp\!\left[-(\sigma/\sigma_0)^m\right]. \]

High \(m\): narrow, predictable strength. Low \(m\): wide, unpredictable strength.

What this lesson assumes

  • Flaws and stress concentration (Lesson 7) — the same idea, applied to a material with no ductility to blunt it.
  • Basic probability (reading a cumulative distribution).
  • Composite behavior as directional, unlike the isotropic metals of Lessons 1–7.
